0.body: Revolutionizing Peripheral Emulation An Applied Sciences R&D project for Deep Tech hardware validation. MIMIC is a hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) emulation system that allows developers to simulate complex sensors and peripherals using standard STM32 hardware.
0.title: MIMIC System

1.body: Zero-Latency Emulation : High-speed SPI and I2C slave simulation. Protocol Bridge : Transparent UART to hardware peripheral mapping. Deep Diagnostics : Real-time pin state and protocol analysis. Extensible API : Python-based sensor modeling. Get Started | Command Reference | Hardware Guide
1.title: Core Capabilities

10.body: Interrupt Managed : Keyboard input is handled via USART1_IRQHandler. Blocking Transactions : Peripheral actions (I2C/SPI) are performed in blocking mode to ensure data integrity before responding to the PC. Stateless logic : The firmware maintains the status of initialized peripherals but treats every GPIO command as an atomic operation.
10.title: Layer 1: The C-Engine (Firmware)

11.body: Auto-Discovery : Scans all COM/tty ports, sending a VERSION challenge to identify the Mimic signature. Command Serialization : Converts high-level Python method calls into the exact string syntax required by the C-Engine. Abstraction Layer : Provides specialized classes like MPU6050Simulator which act as state machines on the PC side, reacting to hardware events reported by the STM32.
11.title: Layer 2: The Python-Shell (MimicLib)

12.body: While the STM32 can toggle pins in nanoseconds, the bottleneck is the 115200 Baud link . Commands take approx 1-2ms to transmit and process. For high-speed logic, the "Sensor Mocking" mode is preferred, as it allows the STM32 to stretch the I2C clock and wait for the Python handler to respond.
12.title: ⚡ Real-time Performance

13.body: This document lists every supported command in Mimic v1. All commands are case-insensitive and must end with \r\n.
13.title: 📜 Command Encyclopedia

15.body: Configures the specified pin as a Push-Pull Output. Example : PIN_SET_OUT A5 Response : OK: Configured A5 as OUTPUT
15.title: PIN_SET_OUT <PIN>

16.body: Configures the specified pin as an Input. PULL Options : UP, DOWN, NONE (default: NONE) Example : PIN_SET_IN B1 UP
16.title: PIN_SET_IN <PIN> [PULL]

17.body: Controls the digital state of the pin. Example : PIN_HIGH C13 (Turns off the onboard LED on most BlackPills)
17.title: PIN_HIGH <PIN> / PIN_LOW <PIN> / PIN_TOGGLE <PIN>

18.body: Returns the current digital state (0 or 1).
18.title: PIN_READ <PIN>

2.body: Follow these steps to develop and test with Mimic.
2.breadcrumbs: Getting Started » Mimic User Start Guide
2.title: Mimic User Start Guide

20.body: Initializes a UART peripheral. Support instances: 1 (usually host), 2, 6. Example : UART_INIT 6 9600
20.title: UART_INIT <INSTANCE> <BAUD>

21.body: Enables automatic Data Enable (DE) management for RS485 transceivers. The DE_PIN will go HIGH during transmission and LOW immediately after the last bit is sent. Example : UART_RS485 6 A15
21.title: UART_RS485 <INSTANCE> <DE_PIN>

24.body: MASTER Mode : I2C_INIT 1 MASTER 100000 (init at 100kHz) SLAVE Mode : I2C_INIT 1 SLAVE 0x68 (init as slave at 0x68)
24.title: I2C_INIT <INSTANCE> <MODE> <SPEED|ADDR>

26.body: Reads <LEN> bytes from the master or slave.
26.title: I2C_READ <INSTANCE> <ADDR> <LEN>

28.body: Displays a technical summary of the system, including: Active GPIO clocks. Peripherals currently initialized. Core clock frequency.
28.title: STATUS

29.body: Performs a software reset of the STM32 (NVIC_SystemReset). Useful for starting fresh without unplugging the USB.
29.title: RESET

3.body:
Use the arduino-cli binary in the project root. Compile & Upload: ./bin/arduino-cli compile --fqbn esp8266:esp8266:nodemcuv2 Esp-Test/MPU6050.ino && \
./bin/arduino-cli upload -p /dev/ttyUSB0 --fqbn esp8266:esp8266:nodemcuv2 Esp-Test/MPU6050.ino Monitor Output: ./bin/arduino-cli monitor -p /dev/ttyUSB0 --config baudrate=115200 Note: Close the monitor (Ctrl+C) before uploading new code.
3.breadcrumbs: Getting Started » 1. Programming the User Device (ESP8266)
3.title: 1. Programming the User Device (ESP8266)

30.body: This guide describes the physical interface of the Mimic board and safety precautions.
30.title: 🔌 BlackPill Hardware Guide

32.body: 3V3 : Output from the onboard regulator or Input if powering externally. Max 3.6V. 5V : Power input (from USB or external). Do not connect to STM32 I/O pins. GND : Common ground reference.
32.title: Power Pins

33.body: Pin Function 5V Tolerant? Notes PA9 UART1 TX YES Host Interface (Connect to Adapter RX) PA10 UART1 RX YES Host Interface (Connect to Adapter TX) PB8 I2C1 SCL YES Primary I2C Clock PB9 I2C1 SDA YES Primary I2C Data PC13 Onboard LED NO Active LOW (Low = ON)
33.title: Critical Peripheral Pins

35.body: Many pins on the STM32F411 are 5V tolerant (marked FT in the datasheet). However, PA4, PA5, and PB5 are NOT 5V tolerant on some packages. Safest Practice : Always use 3.3V logic or level shifters when talking to 5V systems.
35.title: 1. 5V Tolerance

36.body: Each GPIO can source/sink approximately 20mA . Warning : Do not drive motors, solenoids, or high-power LEDs directly from the pins. Always use a MOSFET or transistor.
36.title: 2. Output Current

37.body: If you implement ADC commands, remember the range is 0V to 3.3V . Voltages above 3.3V will permanently damage the ADC circuitry.
37.title: 3. ADC Range

38.body: Board not detected : Ensure the Blue LED is on. This indicates the 3.3V regulator is working. No Serial Response : Swap TX and RX wires. Check that your baud rate is exactly 115200. Stuck in Loop : Press the NRST (Reset) button on the board to restart the firmware.
38.title: 💡 Troubleshooting

39.body: This guide ensures reliable electrical connection between Mimic (STM32) and your User Device (ESP8266/Arduino).
39.title: Mimic Wiring Guide

4.body: On your laptop, the mimic command acts as the "Brain" for the box. Run MPU6050: sudo mimic mpu6050
4.breadcrumbs: Getting Started » 2. Starting the Sensor Simulation
4.title: 2. Starting the Sensor Simulation

40.body: CRITICAL : You MUST connect a GND pin on the STM32 to a GND pin on the User Device. Without this, signal levels will float and data will be corrupted.
40.breadcrumbs: Wiring Diagrams » Ground Rule #1: Shared Ground
40.title: Ground Rule #1: Shared Ground

41.body: The MPU6050 is emulated on I2C Instance 1. STM32 Pin Function User Device Pin PB6 SCL (Clock) SCL (ESP8266 D1 / GPIO5) PB7 SDA (Data) SDA (ESP8266 D2 / GPIO4) GND Ground GND Note on Resistors : Our firmware is optimized to work without resistors at 100kHz, but if you experience "Bus Errors," add a 4.7kΩ resistor from SDA to 3.3V and SCL to 3.3V.
41.title: 1. MPU6050 (I2C) Wiring

42.body: The BMP280 uses the same pins as the MPU6050 but at a different address (0x76). STM32 Pin Function User Device Pin PB6 SCL (Clock) SCL PB7 SDA (Data) SDA GND Ground GND
42.title: 2. BMP280 (I2C) Wiring

43.body: When emulated, SPI follows this standard mapping: STM32 Pin Function User Device Pin PA5 SCK (Clock) SCK PA6 MISO (Out) MISO PA7 MOSI (In) MOSI PA4 CS (Select) CS / SS GND Ground GND
43.title: 3. General SPI Wiring (Preview)

44.body: MIMIC provides a high-level command interface to manipulate GPIO pins on the STM32 hardware.
44.title: GPIO Manipulation

45.body: Pins are referenced by their Port and Number. A5 -> Port A, Pin 5 B12 -> Port B, Pin 12 C13 -> Port C, Pin 13 (Built-in LED)
45.title: Pin Naming Convention

47.body:
Configures a pin to drive external components. PIN_SET_OUT <PIN>
# Example
PIN_SET_OUT A5
47.title: Set as Output

48.body:
Configures a pin to read digital signals. PIN_SET_IN <PIN> [PULL]
# Example
PIN_SET_IN B0 UP # Pull-up resistor enabled
PIN_SET_IN B0 DOWN # Pull-down resistor enabled
PIN_SET_IN B0 NONE # Floating input
48.title: Set as Input

5.body:
If you modify the C code in firmware/Core/Src/mimic.c, you must re-flash the hardware. Flash via ST-Link: cd firmware
make clean && make flash
5.breadcrumbs: Getting Started » 3. Updating the Mimic Box (STM32)
5.title: 3. Updating the Mimic Box (STM32)

54.body:
PIN_READ <PIN>
# or short command
PR <PIN> Response: HIGH or LOW.
54.title: Read Logic Level

56.body: Manually set the hardware mode of the pin. PIN_MODE <PIN> <IN|OUT|AF|AN> IN: Input OUT: Output AF: Alternate Function (for I2C, SPI, etc.) AN: Analog (for ADC/DAC)
56.title: Pin Mode

57.body:
View the complete internal configuration of a pin. PIN_STATUS <PIN>
# or short command
PS <PIN> Example Output: PIN A5: Mode: OUTPUT Type: PUSH_PULL Speed: LOW Pull: NONE State: LOW
57.title: Pin Status

58.body: Detailed technical specifications for communication protocols supported by Mimic v1.
58.title: 🧬 Protocol Specifications

6.body: "No Mimic board found" : Unplug and re-plug the STM32 USB cable. It may have crashed the serial bridge or changed port names. "Device or resource busy" : A Serial Monitor is already open. Close it. Constant -57.80 readings : Signal wires are loose or disconnected. Mimic is serving "Ghost Data."
6.title: 4. Common Troubleshooting

60.body: Mimic uses the STM32 Hardware I2C peripheral. Standard Mode : 100 kHz (Recommended) Fast Mode : Up to 400 kHz (Supported, but stability depends on cable length and pull-ups). Slave Mode : Supports clock stretching. If the Python host is slow to respond, the STM32 will hold the SCL line LOW to pause the master.
60.title: Implementation

61.body: I2C1 : SCL (PB8), SDA (PB9) Voltage : 3.3V logic. Pull-ups : The STM32 enables internal pull-ups, but for reliable communication (especially at 400kHz), external 4.7kΩ resistors to 3.3V are strongly recommended.
61.title: Wiring

63.body: Clock Polarity (CPOL) : 0 or 1 Clock Phase (CPHA) : 0 or 1 Data Size : 8-bit or 16-bit Bit Order : MSB First (Standard) or LSB First.
63.title: Implementation

64.body: SPI1 : SCK (PA5), MISO (PA6), MOSI (PA7). CS : Any GPIO can be used as Chip Select via the SPI_CS command.
64.title: Wiring

66.body: The RS485 implementation is hardware-assisted. When UART_SEND is called, the DE pin is pulled HIGH. The UART data is streamed. The firmware waits for the TC (Transmission Complete) flag in the hardware register to ensure the last bit has physically left the pin. The DE pin is pulled LOW. This ensures zero-latency switching between transmit and receive modes, essential for half-duplex RS485 communication.
66.title: Hardware DE Management

67.body: One of the most powerful features of Mimic is the ability to write your own sensor simulation classes. This allows you to "mock" any I2C or SPI device.
67.breadcrumbs: Sensors API » 🛠 Sensors API: Writing Your Own Mock
67.title: 🛠 Sensors API: Writing Your Own Mock

68.body:
New sensors should be placed in mimic/sensors/ and should follow this pattern: import logging
from mimic.bridge import I2CSlave, MimicBridge class MyNewSensor: def __init__(self, bridge: MimicBridge, address: int): self.i2c = I2CSlave(bridge) self.address = address # 1. Define your internal registers self.registers = { 0x01: [0x55], # Status Reg 0x02: [0x00, 0x00] # Data Reg } def start(self): # 2. Init the hardware self.i2c.init(self.address) while True: # 3. Wait for the Master to talk received = self.i2c.wait_for_write(1) if received: reg = received[0] # Handle Writes if len(received) > 1: self.registers[reg] = received[1:] continue # Handle Reads data = self.registers.get(reg, [0xFF]) self.i2c.respond(data)
68.title: 🧱 The Simulator Structure

69.body: Block Sizes : Keep your internal register lists as byte arrays. Clock Stretching : The wait_for_write method is blocking. This is good because it triggers I2C clock stretching on the STM32, ensuring the master waits for your Python code to process the logic. Real-time Data : You can update your internal registers from a separate thread or based on another input, and the Master will get the "live" value on the next I2C read.
69.title: 📋 Best Practices

7.body: Mimic v1 is built on a Command-Response Bridge architecture. It transforms complex, low-level hardware timings into human-readable string transactions.
7.title: 🏛 Architecture Overview

70.body: To make your new sensor available in the mimic-sim command, add it to the choice list in mimic/cli.py.
70.title: 🚀 Adding to CLI

71.body: Mimic uses an STM32F401 (Black Pill) coupled with a Python-based Host Bridge. While powerful, it has specific physical and timing constraints.
71.title: Mimic System Limitations

72.body: Max Speed (Standard) : 100 kHz. Max Speed (Fast) : 400 kHz (Requires physical 4.7kΩ pull-up resistors). No-Resistor Operation : Stable at 100 kHz due to firmware-level interrupt optimization, but requires short jumper wires (< 15cm). Slave Address : Supports any 7-bit address (configured via simulation script).
72.title: 1. I2C (Inter-Integrated Circuit)

73.body: Mode : Slave Only. Max Clock : 18 MHz (Tested). Constraints : Requires a dedicated Chip Select (CS) pin. Extremely sensitive to wire length at speeds above 1MHz.
73.title: 2. SPI (Serial Peripheral Interface)

74.body: Standard Baudrate : 115200 bps. Update Latency : ~1.5ms per register update. Buffer Depth : 256-byte Shadow Register Map. The STM32 serves data instantly from RAM, but updates from the Laptop are subject to OS serial scheduling.
74.title: 3. Host-to-Mimic Bridge (Serial)

75.body: Logic Level : 3.3V Only. Connecting to 5V devices (like older Arduino Unos) requires a logic level shifter. Internal Pull-ups : ~40kΩ. Significant for I2C; not recommended for high-speed operation without external support.
75.title: 4. Hardware Constraints

76.body: Mimic v1 is built on a Command-Response Bridge architecture. It transforms complex, low-level hardware timings into human-readable string transactions.
76.title: 🏛 Architecture Overview

77.body: Host Request : The PC (Python) sends a UTF-8 string over UART (e.g., PIN_HIGH A5\r\n). Firmware Parsing : The STM32 receives this via an interrupt-driven buffer. It tokensizes the command and arguments. Action Execution : The internal mimic.c engine calls the relevant HAL function (e.g., HAL_GPIO_WritePin). Hardware Response : The STM32 captures the result (Success, Data, or Error Code). Host Response : The STM32 sends back a structured response string (e.g., OK: Pin A5 set HIGH\r\n> ).
77.title: 🔄 The Data Flow

79.body: Interrupt Managed : Keyboard input is handled via USART1_IRQHandler. Blocking Transactions : Peripheral actions (I2C/SPI) are performed in blocking mode to ensure data integrity before responding to the PC. Stateless logic : The firmware maintains the status of initialized peripherals but treats every GPIO command as an atomic operation.
79.title: Layer 1: The C-Engine (Firmware)

It tokensizes the command and arguments. Action Execution : The internal mimic.c engine calls the relevant HAL function (e.g., HAL_GPIO_WritePin). Hardware Response : The STM32 captures the result (Success, Data, or Error Code). Host Response : The STM32 sends back a structured response string (e.g., OK: Pin A5 set HIGH\r\n> ).
8.title: 🔄 The Data Flow
